reasoning: The effective federal funds rate (DFF) has sat perfectly flat at 4.33% since January 2025. Given the abundance of reserves, the DFF does not experience positive day-to-day upward technical volatility, effectively pegging it to 4.33% absent a Fed rate hike. The Federal Reserve is broadly anticipated to ease later this year, with highly authoritative market pricing (CME FedWatch based on fed funds futures) giving >85% probability of a 25 basis point or larger rate cut at the September 2025 FOMC meeting. The FOMC does not meet before the first resolution date (Sept 7), meaning the rate will almost universally remain at 4.33% barring an unprecedented inter-meeting hike. Across upcoming resolution horizons, the dominant expectation is cuts or holds. Thus, for the rate to be strictly 'increased' above 4.33%, it would require a severe unexpected resurgence in inflation forcing the Fed to reverse course and hike rates. The probability of this occurring scales gradually with the time horizon but remains well below 10% even through Q1 2026 due to current weak economic data (e.g., July's 73,000 nonfarm payroll print).
